1: The Lake of Gennesaret (= Sea of Galilee / Kinneret) is identified by this name in Josephus (War 3.463; Ant. 5.84) and 1 Macc Luke 11:67. The fishing industry on the lake employed a significant portion of the Galilean labor force; archaeological evidence at Capernaum, Bethsaida, and Magdala includes fish-processing installations, net weights, and a first-century fishing boat (the "Jesus Boat," found in 1986). The lake is ca. 21 km long and 13 km wide at its widest, lying 213 m below sea level. Commercial fishing was organized around partnerships (koinōnoi, Luke 5:10) and involved significant capital investment in boats and nets.
2: Two boats (ploiaria) were standing at the shore while the fishermen washed their nets. Net-washing after the night's fishing removed weeds, debris, and small fish; the nets were then dried and repaired before the next outing. Night fishing was standard on the lake (John 21:3-4) -- warmer water drove fish deeper during the day, while cooler night temperatures brought them to the surface. The return of fishermen after an unsuccessful night of work establishes the miracle's setting: this is not casual fishing but professional effort that has yielded nothing.
3: Jesus uses Simon's boat as a floating teaching platform -- a practical solution to crowd pressure from the lake shore. The rabbis taught in synagogues, temple courts, and outdoors; teaching from a boat was unusual but not unprecedented. The crowd's pressing (epēkonto) reflects Luke's consistent portrait of popular enthusiasm for Jesus' teaching. The detail that Jesus "sat down" (kathisas) to teach confirms the seated posture of authoritative Jewish teaching (cf. Luke 4:20; Matthew 5:1; Luke 23:2).
4: "Put out into deep water" (epānagagē eis to bathos) -- the deep water (bathos) is the middle of the lake, away from the shallows where daytime fishing was possible. The instruction reverses normal fishing practice: go deeper in the daylight when fish are not available. Jesus addresses Simon directly and specifically, anticipating his leadership role. Simon's response (Luke 5:5) combines professional skepticism with personal submission -- he knows the lake conditions, but he will comply on the basis of Jesus' word (epi de tō rhēmati sou).
5: Simon's response "Master" (Epistatē) is a Lukan title for Jesus used by disciples and petitioners (Luke 8:24, 45; Luke 9:33, 49; Luke 17:13) -- different from "rabbi" or "teacher" and unique to Luke among the Gospels. It carries the sense of overseer, commander, or one in charge. The acknowledgment "we worked hard all night and haven't caught anything" establishes both the professional exhaustion and the impossibility of further success. "But because you say so, I will let down the nets" is the pivot of faith: compliance without rational expectation.
6: The miraculous catch (icthyon plethos poly = a great multitude of fish) was so large that the nets began to tear (dierrēgnuto). The scale is deliberately excessive -- this is not a good day's catch but an impossible haul. The miracle echoes Ezekiel's vision of the restored fishing industry (Ezekiel 47:10: "fishermen will stand along the shore; there will be places for spreading nets"). In the post-resurrection parallel (John 21:1-14), the same miracle is repeated: 153 large fish, the nets not breaking. The Lukan episode functions as a calling narrative (the catch = the future mission harvest).
7: The partners (metochoi, a term for business co-partners) in the other boat are signaled for help. The partnership structure (koinōnia) of Galilean fishing was economically sophisticated: boats, nets, and labor were shared across family and business networks. James and John, sons of Zebedee, are named as Simon's partners -- establishing the network of relationships before the formal call of Luke 5:10-11. Both boats fill to sinking (hōste bythizesthai auta = so that they were beginning to sink) -- the miraculous abundance threatens to consume the very vessels it fills, a symbol of the mission's overwhelming scope.
8: "Go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man!" (Exelthe ap' emou, hoti anēr hamartolos eimi, Kyrie) -- Simon's response is the OT pattern of the creature encountering the holy: Isaiah's "Woe to me, I am ruined" at the seraphim vision (Isaiah 6:5), Gideon's "I will die for I have seen the angel of the Lord face to face" (Judges 6:22), and Ezekiel's prostration (Ezekiel 1:28). The awareness of unworthiness before holiness is not false modesty but the creaturely recognition of the chasm between human sinfulness and divine presence. Simon's use of Kyrios (Lord) here is Luke's first application of the resurrection title to the earthly Jesus in this context.
9: The astonishment (thambos = amazement, awe) that seized Simon and all his companions was caused by the catch -- not merely wonder at a large haul but the recognition that this is a supernatural sign. Thambos in Luke appears at divine encounters (Luke 4:36; Luke 5:9) and is the Lukan equivalent of the OT fear of God (yir'ah) at theophanic moments. The event crosses the line between remarkable fortune and divine intervention -- the fishermen know the lake well enough to recognize that what happened was categorically impossible by natural causes.
10: James and John, the sons of Zebedee (with their business partners), will form with Simon the inner circle of disciples (Luke 8:51; Luke 9:28). The family enterprise of Zebedee appears in Mark 1:20 to employ hired workers, suggesting a prosperous operation. Jesus' word to Simon -- "from now on you will catch people" (zōgrōn esē, present participle = catching alive) -- reworks the fishing metaphor into a mission metaphor: zōgreō means literally "to catch alive/take alive" (used of capturing warriors alive rather than killing them, 2 Timothy 2:26). The future mission will not kill but give life.
11: "They pulled their boats up on shore, left everything, and followed him" -- the abandonment of the boats, nets, and the largest catch of their lives is the radical detachment that characterizes the disciple-call in all three Synoptics (Mark 1:18, 20; Matthew 4:20, 22). Luke adds "left everything" (aphentes panta) -- the absolute renunciation formula that will appear again with Levi (Luke 5:28) and the rich ruler (Luke 18:23). In an honor-shame society, the boats and nets represented a family's entire capital and social identity; to leave them was to abandon economic security and social standing for an itinerant, homeless ministry.
12: Leprosy (lepra in NT Greek) was not necessarily Hansen's disease but a category covering various chronic skin conditions defined by the Levitical purity code (Lev 13-14). The social consequences were severe: the leper was declared "unclean" by the priest, required to live outside the community, tear their clothes, keep their hair disheveled, cover their mouth, and call "Unclean! Unclean!" when others approached (Leviticus 13:45-46). Social isolation combined with economic inability meant lepers depended entirely on charity. The man in Luke is "covered with leprosy" (plērēs lepras = full of leprosy), indicating advanced disease affecting much of his body.
13: Jesus "reached out his hand and touched the man" -- the touch is the most provocative element. By touching a leper, Jesus contracted ritual uncleanness (Leviticus 5:3). That Jesus touches first and commands "be clean" (katharisthēti) simultaneously illustrates the reversal at the heart of his ministry: rather than uncleanness transferring from the leper to the clean Jesus, Jesus' purity overcomes the uncleanness. The cleansing was immediate (eutheōs = at once). The parallel in 2 Kings 5:11 (Naaman expected Elisha to wave his hand over the leprosy) highlights the personal, physical intimacy of Jesus' healing over Elisha's distance-healing.
14: "Don't tell anyone" -- the privacy instruction is paradoxical given the public nature of the healing. The instruction to "show yourself to the priest" follows Leviticus 14:2-32 exactly: the cleansed person must present themselves to the priest, who examines the skin and performs the specified purification ritual over eight days with two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop. This is expensive and time-consuming -- Jesus insists on full Torah compliance, not a shortcut. "As a testimony to them" (eis martyrion autois) means testimony to the priests of Jesus' Torah faithfulness, and to the community that the man is genuinely restored to full social and religious participation.
15: The withdrawal pattern appears here as a response to growing fame. "News about him spread all the more" (diercheto de mallon ho logos) is Lukan irony: the instruction to silence increases the report. Crowds gathered from all directions to hear Jesus and to be healed -- Luke uses "wilderness" (erēmous topous = desolate places) as the retreat location, linking the prayer-withdrawal to the post-baptism wilderness (Luke 4:1) and anticipating Gethsemane (Luke 22:39-44). The crowds that pursue Jesus into the wilderness also echo the manna-wilderness: the one who refuses to turn stones into bread nonetheless will feed a multitude (Luke 9:10-17).
16: "Jesus often withdrew to lonely places and prayed" -- Luke's prayer summary verses punctuate the narrative at moments of transition or mounting pressure: before the baptism (Luke 3:21), after the leper healing (Luke 5:16), before choosing the Twelve (Luke 6:12), before Peter's confession (Luke 9:18), at the Transfiguration (Luke 9:28), teaching the Lord's Prayer (Luke 11:1), and Gethsemane (Luke 22:41). The pattern of withdrawing from demanding ministry to re-engage the Father reflects Luke's portrayal of Jesus as the model for Christian prayer -- the community's prayer practice (Acts 1:14; Luke 2:42; Luke 4:24-31) mirrors Jesus' own habit.
17: The formal opposition makes its first appearance: Pharisees and teachers of the law (nomodidaskaloi = law-teachers, a Lukan word also at Acts 5:34 and 1 Timothy 1:7) came from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem. The geographical scope signals a coordinated response -- this is not local curiosity but a deputation. The Pharisees were a reform movement within Judaism (ca. 150 BCE - 70 CE) focused on precise Torah observance and the development of the Oral Torah. The scribes/teachers of the law (grammateis / nomodidaskaloi) were professional Torah interpreters who made legal rulings. Together they represent the official interpretive establishment. "The power of the Lord was with him to heal" (Luke 5:17b) is Luke's narrative signal that what follows will demonstrate divine authority challenging their authority.
18: Palestinian domestic architecture in the first century used flat clay roofs supported by wooden beams. Luke uses keramōn (tiles) -- either describing a Greek-style tiled roof or adapting Mark's description for his Hellenistic audience (Mark 2:4 specifies "they dug through the roof"). The effort of four men carrying a pallet (klinidion = small bed/mat) up exterior stairs and through the roof to lower it into a crowded room demonstrates extraordinary determination -- a determination Jesus interprets as "faith" (Luke 5:20). The paralytic (paralelymenos = loosened/broken in limb) was unable to act on his own; his friends' faith mediates for him.
19: The courtyard scenario in a typical Galilean house: the crowd fills both the room where Jesus teaches and the doorway, making entry impossible. The friends carry the paralytic up to the flat roof (exterior stairs were common), remove part of the roof, and lower him on his mat into the open space in front of Jesus. Luke's keramōn may reflect that he describes this for readers who know tiled Mediterranean roofs better than clay Palestinian roofs. The lowering through the roof and into the middle (eis to meson) of the crowd before Jesus is a bold act that cannot be ignored.
20: "Friend, your sins are forgiven" (Anthrōpe, apheontai soi hai hamartiai sou) -- Jesus addresses the man directly and personally (anthrōpe is a term of familiar address). The pronouncement of forgiveness before the healing makes the healing secondary -- it is the spiritual state, not the physical, that Jesus addresses first. Aphesis (forgiveness/release) is a Jubilee word (Leviticus 25:10 LXX; Isaiah 61:1) -- the same lexical field as "release to the captives" in Luke 4:18. The connection between sin and physical suffering was a common assumption in Second Temple Judaism (John 9:2: "who sinned, this man or his parents?"), though Jesus will challenge it (Luke 13:2-5).
21: "Who can forgive sins but God alone?" (tis dynatai aphienai hamartias ei mē monos ho theos?) -- the theological objection is correct within the framework of Second Temple Jewish monotheism. Only God can forgive sins against God -- and all sins are ultimately against God (Psalms 51:4: "Against you, you only, have I sinned"). The scribes and Pharisees are not theologically confused; their objection accurately represents the Jewish understanding of divine prerogative. What they are unprepared for is the possibility that the one who stands before them has divine authority. The charge of blasphemy was a capital offence (Leviticus 24:16; m. Sanhedrin Luke 7:5) -- a charge that will return at the trial (Luke 22:66-71).
22: Jesus' knowledge of the scribes' inner dialogue (dialogismoi = calculations/reasonings) is presented as a claim to divine insight. In Jewish thought, God alone knows the heart (1 Samuel 16:7; 1 Kings 8:39; Psalms 139:1-4; Jeremiah 17:10). The question "Which is easier -- to say 'Your sins are forgiven' or to say 'Get up and walk'?" is a rhetorical trap: both are impossible without divine authority, but the first is unverifiable while the second is immediately testable. Jesus will use the testable miracle to authenticate the untestable forgiveness -- the physical healing as sign of the spiritual reality.
23: "That you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" -- the Son of Man (ho Huios tou anthrōpou) title is used here for the first time in Luke (subsequent uses: Luke 6:5, 22; Luke 7:34; Luke 9:22, 26, 44, 58; Luke 11:30; Luke 12:8, 10, 40; Luke 17:22-30; Luke 18:8, 31; Luke 19:10; Luke 21:27, 36; Luke 22:22, 48, 69; Luke 24:7). The title derives from Daniel 7:13-14 (one "like a son of man" receives authority from the Ancient of Days) and was used in Jewish apocalyptic for a heavenly figure (1 Enoch 46-48; 4 Ezra 13). Jesus applies it to himself in an earthly, present context -- the exousia delegated to the heavenly Son of Man is exercised on earth.
24: "Get up, take your mat and go home" -- the three-fold command demonstrates complete restoration: the paralytic stands (anistathai = rise), takes the mat that was his disability marker, and walks home. The reversal is complete: the man who was carried in on a mat now carries the mat out under his own power. In the context of Luke 5:17 (the Pharisees observing with critical intent), the physical miracle serves as the divine authentication of the forgiveness claim -- it answers the unverifiable with the undeniable. The public setting ensures this cannot be dismissed as private suggestibility.
25: The formerly paralyzed man goes home "praising God" (doxazon ton theon) -- the standard Lukan response to miraculous events (Luke 5:26; Luke 7:16; Luke 13:13; Luke 17:15; Luke 18:43; Luke 23:47). The community response (Luke 5:26) adds "filled with awe and praised God" -- the Greek ekplexis (overwhelm/stun) and the doxological response together constitute the appropriate human reaction to the in-breaking of divine power in the healing/forgiveness complex.
26: "We have seen remarkable things today" (eidamen paradoxa sēmeron) -- the crowd's response uses paradoxa, a word Luke uses only here in the NT, meaning things that violate normal expectation, marvels. The community is overwhelmed (ekplexis = struck out of normal consciousness) and their praise goes to God (edoxazon ton theon). The Pharisees' silent theological objection (Luke 5:21) is swamped by the crowd's vocal praise -- the people have drawn the correct inference from the miracle even if the authorities have not.
27: Levi the tax collector was sitting at his tax booth (telōnion) -- the customs post, likely at Capernaum on the Via Maris trade route or at the border between Herod Antipas's Galilee and Philip's territory. Tax collectors (telōnai) in the first century were local contractors who purchased the right to collect tolls and customs from the Roman or Herodian administration, keeping any excess above the contracted amount as profit. The system incentivized extortion and made the telōnai socially despised -- associated with Gentile collaboration, financial dishonesty, and ritual impurity from handling Gentile money. Levi is also called Matthew (Mark 2:14 is also "Levi son of Alphaeus"; some identify him with Matthew the apostle).
28: "Leaving everything" (katalipōn panta) -- the same phrase used of the fishermen (aphentes panta, Luke 5:11). The tax collector's abandonment is even more dramatic: the fishermen left boats and nets, which they could in principle return to (cf. John 21:3). Levi leaves the telōnion, the source of income, the contracted right, the established livelihood -- to follow an itinerant teacher with no fixed abode. The parallel structure of the disciple-calls (fishermen + tax collector) signals that the community around Jesus will include socially diverse members -- manual laborers and financial collaborators alike.
29: Levi's great banquet (dochēn megalēn) for Jesus at his house drew "a large crowd of tax collectors and others" -- the social network of a tax collector would naturally include other telōnai, merchants, and those on the social margins of respectable Jewish society. Table fellowship (commensality) was the primary mechanism of social boundary maintenance in Mediterranean culture: you ate with your social equals, your family, and those you included in your community. Jesus' recumbence at table with tax collectors and "sinners" (hamartolos = those who either lived immorally or practiced occupations considered incompatible with Torah) is a deliberate, visible boundary-crossing.
30: "The Pharisees and the teachers of the law who belonged to their sect complained to his disciples" (egongyzon) -- egongyzō is the LXX verb for Israel's grumbling in the wilderness (Exodus 15:24; Luke 16:7-8; Luke 17:3; Numbers 11:1; Luke 14:2; cf. 1 Corinthians 10:10). Luke uses this specific word at Luke 5:30, Luke 15:2, and 19:7 in connection with table-fellowship controversies involving tax collectors and sinners -- deliberately evoking the wilderness murmuring tradition. The community around Jesus is the new exodus people, and the opposition's complaint is the same old wilderness rebellion against God's provision.
31: "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick" -- the physician metaphor is fitting both for Luke (Colossians 4:14) and for the social context: the comparison of the teacher/healer to a physician is attested in Hellenistic moral philosophy (Plutarch uses it of Socrates; Epictetus compares his school to a physician's surgery). The metaphor reframes the table-fellowship controversy: the "sinners" are the sick patients, not the healthy excluded. The physician's presence among the sick is not contamination but treatment.
32: "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance" (eis metanoian) -- metanoia (repentance, turning) is a Lukan keyword: Luke 3:3 (John's baptism of repentance), Luke 3:8 (fruit worthy of repentance), Luke 5:32, Luke 10:13, Luke 11:32, Luke 13:3, 5; Luke 15:7, 10; Luke 16:30; Luke 17:3-4; Luke 24:47. Repentance in Luke is not merely sorrow for sin but a comprehensive re-orientation of life -- the prodigal son "coming to himself" and returning to the father (Luke 15:17-20). The call of Levi and the subsequent banquet is simultaneously a call to repentance and its immediate expression in table fellowship.
33: The question about fasting contrasts John's disciples and the Pharisees' disciples with Jesus' disciples. Jewish fasting practices in the Second Temple period: the Pharisees fasted twice a week (Monday and Thursday, Didache 8:1 criticizes "hypocrites" for this); the Day of Atonement was the only biblically mandated fast (Leviticus 16:29-34); voluntary fasting was practiced for mourning (2 Samuel 12:16), repentance (Jonah 3:5; 1 Kings 21:27), and prophetic preparation (Daniel 10:3). The observation that Jesus' disciples neither fast nor pray like others suggests the disciples' relative informality about structured pious practice.
34: "Can you make the friends of the bridegroom fast while he is with them?" -- the wedding metaphor is eschatological: the kingdom of God is consistently compared to a wedding banquet in the Synoptics (Matthew 22:2-14; Luke 25:1-13; Luke 14:16-24). The bridegroom's presence is the condition of celebration, not mourning -- and Jesus is the bridegroom. The metaphor implicitly identifies Jesus with the divine bridegroom of the OT (God as husband of Israel: Isaiah 54:5; Luke 62:5; Jeremiah 31:32; Ezek 16; Hosea 2:19-20). Fasting is the practice of absence and longing; feasting is the practice of presence and joy.
35: "The time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; in those days they will fast" -- the first explicit Lukan passion prediction, veiled in the wedding metaphor. "Taken from them" (aparthē) echoes Isaiah 53:8 LXX: "his life was taken away from the earth." The future fasting of the disciples points to the period after the crucifixion-ascension and before the parousia -- the Church's present age. The early church did fast (Acts 13:2-3; Luke 14:23; Didache 8), and this verse provides the theological rationale.
36: The parable of the new patch on an old garment inverts normal clothing repair logic: no one cuts new cloth to patch old -- the new piece would shrink, tear the old, and the patch would not match anyway. The incompatibility of new and old in this application: Jesus' teaching cannot be simply appended to the old religious framework. It requires new fabric -- a new structure, not a patch on the old. The point is not that the old (Torah, covenant, tradition) is worthless but that the new wine of the kingdom cannot be contained within the forms developed for a different age.
37: "And no one pours new wine into old wineskins" -- new wine continues to ferment and expand; the already-stretched old skins (dried leather bottles) cannot absorb the pressure and will burst. The new skins have enough elasticity to accommodate the expanding wine. The parable extends the previous image: the kingdom's dynamic reality requires new forms adequate to its character. Luke's context in the fasting controversy: the old forms of Pharisaic piety (regular fasting, ritual prayer) are the old wineskins; the new reality of Jesus' presence demands forms that can accommodate it.
38: "New wine must be poured into new wineskins" -- the positive conclusion of the previous negative examples. The new wine is the dynamic reality of the kingdom; the new wineskins are the forms adequate to it -- table fellowship with sinners, joyful celebration in the bridegroom's presence, the freedom of the physician among the sick. The imagery was familiar to any Mediterranean audience: leather wine containers (askos = wineskin) were a common household item, and the behavior of new wine in old containers was a practical reality.
39: "And no one after drinking old wine wants the new, for they say, 'The old is better.'" -- this final saying, unique to Luke, adds a psychologically realistic coda: people resist the new not because it is inferior but because they are accustomed to the old. The conservative preference for the familiar is a natural human pattern. In the context of the controversy, this explains the Pharisees' resistance: they have been shaped by the old forms and find them satisfying. The saying is not a condemnation but an observation -- a gentle acknowledgment that the transition to the new is genuinely difficult and that human preference does not determine what is actually better.
